feedback.text: Dear European Commission, Thank you for opening up the Digital Fairness Act for public commentary. Id like to respectfully raise an issue that affects a growing number of consumers across the EU: the deliberate shutdown or removal of video games that people have already purchased. In recent years, we've seen a troubling rise in games being rendered unplayable due to online servers being shut down, DRM restrictions, or digital storefronts delisting them entirely. This practice undermines consumer rights. When we buy a game, we expect to be able to use it in the futurejust like we would with a physical product. There is currently no robust legal protection in place to stop publishers from revoking access to digital games after sale, even if there is no technical reason they must be shut down. Hundreds of titles have already been lost forever, and many more are at risksome of which were critically acclaimed or culturally important. I believe the Digital Fairness Act presents an opportunity to address this. Even a simple addition recognizing the need to preserve access to purchased digital goodsespecially gamescould make a substantial difference. It would send a signal that consumer rights in the digital space must be taken seriously, and that permanent loss of purchased content is not acceptable. Please consider adding a provision or recommendation that protects EU consumers from having their digital games revoked or made inaccessible after purchase. Many of us have spent money on games we love, only to lose them due to business decisions beyond our control. This is deeply unfair.
